title: Project initiation
definition: Initiation is the phase where a project is formally authorized and its high-level purpose, boundaries, and leadership are established.
key_points: Formally authorizes the project or phase
Appoints/empowers the project manager (PM) to begin planning
Creates alignment on why the project exists
why_it_matters: Many exam questions test whether you understand what can and cannot be done before authorization—initiation prevents wasted effort and misaligned work.
example: An executive approves launching a new customer portal and assigns a PM, funding, and success criteria before detailed planning begins.
exam_trap: Starting detailed scope, schedule, or procurement planning before the project is authorized.

title: Business case
definition: A business case justifies the project by describing the business need, expected benefits, costs, risks, and alternatives.
key_points: Explains why the project should exist
why_it_matters: PMP questions often ask what document justifies a project and where the charter’s rationale comes from.
example: A hospital estimates reduced readmissions by implementing a new care coordination system; compares build vs buy.
exam_trap: Confusing the business case with the project charter; the charter authorizes, the business case justifies.

title: Benefits management plan
definition: A benefits management plan defines intended benefits, ownership, measurement approach, and timing—often beyond the project’s end date.
key_points: Benefits can be realized after delivery
why_it_matters: PMP scenarios test whether you connect projects to benefits realization, not just deliverables.
example: After deploying a new billing system, finance owns a 12‑month target to reduce claim denials by 15%.
exam_trap: Assuming benefits are realized at project close; deliverables ≠ benefits.

title: Project charter
definition: The project charter is the document that formally authorizes the project and gives the PM authority to apply organizational resources.
key_points: High-level scope, objectives, and success criteria
Names the PM and defines authority level
why_it_matters: Many PMP questions hinge on: 'Do you have a charter?' If not, you’re not authorized to proceed.
example: The sponsor signs the charter stating objective, budget cap, major milestones, and PM authority to staff a team.
exam_trap: Treating the charter like a detailed project management plan; it’s high-level and created early.

title: Charter contents (typical)
definition: A charter typically includes purpose, measurable objectives, high-level requirements, assumptions, constraints, risks, summary schedule and budget, and stakeholder list.
why_it_matters: Exam items may ask which element belongs in the charter vs the management plan.
example: Charter lists budget ≤ $500k, go-live by Q4, and key requirement: HIPAA compliance.
exam_trap: Putting WBS, detailed schedule network, or full risk register in the charter.

title: High-level requirements
definition: High-level requirements describe what the solution must do, stated broadly enough to guide planning without constraining design prematurely.
key_points: Express business needs at a high level
why_it_matters: You’ll be tested on distinguishing high-level requirements (charter) from detailed requirements (requirements documentation).
example: “System must support multi-factor authentication for all staff users.”
exam_trap: Jumping directly to detailed specifications and design choices during initiation.

title: Assumptions
definition: Assumptions are factors considered true for planning purposes, which may later prove false and create risk.
why_it_matters: PMP scenarios often ask what to do when assumptions change—update and manage impacts.
example: Assuming a vendor API will be available by May; if delayed, schedule risk increases.
exam_trap: Treating assumptions as guaranteed facts and ignoring their risk implications.

title: Constraints
definition: Constraints are limiting factors that restrict options, such as fixed deadlines, budgets, resources, regulatory rules, or mandated tools.
key_points: Often set by sponsor or environment
why_it_matters: Constraints show up in many questions about feasibility and trade-offs.
example: A regulatory deadline forces go-live before year-end; overtime budget is capped.
exam_trap: Calling everything a constraint; distinguish constraints from assumptions and preferences.

title: Project selection methods (overview)
definition: Selection methods help organizations choose which projects to authorize based on strategy, value, risk, and constraints.
why_it_matters: PMP commonly tests NPV/IRR/payback and how selection decisions are made.
example: A portfolio team ranks proposals using NPV and risk score to decide which to fund.
exam_trap: Assuming the PM chooses projects; selection is usually portfolio/governance.

title: Net present value (NPV)
definition: NPV is the present value of expected cash inflows minus outflows; positive NPV indicates value creation given the discount rate.
key_points: Accounts for time value of money
Higher NPV generally preferred (all else equal)
why_it_matters: NPV is a frequent exam calculation/interpretation topic.
example: A project with discounted benefits of $1.2M and costs of $900k has NPV = $300k.
exam_trap: Comparing cash flows without discounting, or choosing lower NPV because it’s 'cheaper.'

title: Internal rate of return (IRR)
definition: IRR is the discount rate at which NPV equals zero; projects with IRR above the hurdle rate are usually acceptable.
key_points: Compared to required rate of return
May be less useful for non-standard cash flows
why_it_matters: PMP uses IRR for selection model questions and trade-offs.
example: If hurdle rate is 8% and IRR is 12%, the project is generally attractive.
exam_trap: Assuming higher IRR always best without considering strategic fit or risk.

title: Payback period
definition: Payback period is the time required for cumulative benefits to repay the initial investment.
why_it_matters: Exam questions test method limitations and when to use them.
example: A $200k project returning $50k/month has payback ~4 months (ignoring discounting).
exam_trap: Using payback to pick between projects without considering total value or NPV.

title: Scoring models
definition: Scoring models rank projects against weighted criteria such as strategic alignment, risk, compliance, and ROI.
why_it_matters: PMP questions often ask how to choose between competing projects with different benefits.
example: Compliance gets weight 40%, revenue 30%, risk 30%; projects are scored and ranked.
exam_trap: Thinking scoring replaces governance judgment; it supports decision-making.

title: Constraints vs requirements
definition: Requirements define what must be delivered; constraints limit how/when/with what resources the work can be done.
why_it_matters: Exam items often give statements and ask you to classify them correctly.
example: Requirement: 'support mobile login.' Constraint: 'must use existing SSO provider.'
exam_trap: Labeling a preferred solution as a requirement when it’s actually a constraint or assumption.

title: Sponsor
definition: The sponsor is the person or group that provides resources, champions the project, and authorizes the charter.
why_it_matters: PMP frequently tests roles/responsibilities and escalation paths.
example: A VP sponsors an initiative, approves budget, and clears roadblocks with other departments.
exam_trap: Assuming sponsor manages day-to-day work; that’s the PM’s role.

title: Project manager (PM) authority
definition: PM authority is the level of decision-making power granted to the PM, influenced by the charter and organizational structure.
key_points: Defined/limited by org structure and charter
Affects resource control and decision speed
why_it_matters: Many questions test how org structure affects PM power and how to lead without direct authority.
example: In a functional org, the PM negotiates with department managers for staff time.
exam_trap: Assuming every PM can assign resources directly; only true in stronger matrix/projectized orgs.

title: Functional organization
definition: A functional organization groups staff by specialty; PM authority is typically low and functional managers control resources.
key_points: PM coordinates but lacks direct authority
why_it_matters: Org structure is a recurring PMP topic tied to resource negotiation and conflict.
example: IT, Finance, and HR each allocate partial resources; approvals run through functional managers.
exam_trap: Expecting dedicated team members in a functional org without negotiation.

title: Matrix organization (weak/balanced/strong)
definition: Matrix orgs share power between functional managers and PMs; PM authority varies from weak to strong.
key_points: Weak: PM low authority, FM high
Strong: PM high authority, dedicated resources more common
why_it_matters: PMP questions often give scenarios about conflicts and ask what structure is implied.
example: A strong matrix gives PM budget control and decision rights while functional managers maintain career paths.
exam_trap: Assuming 'matrix' always means high PM authority—weak matrix looks like functional.

title: Projectized organization
definition: In a projectized organization, PMs have high authority and teams are often dedicated full time to projects.
why_it_matters: Exam questions test how authority and resource availability change across structures.
example: A consulting firm assigns teams to projects; PMs control budgets and priorities.
exam_trap: Thinking projectized eliminates all conflict—resources can still be constrained across projects.

title: Stakeholder
definition: A stakeholder is an individual, group, or organization that may affect, be affected by, or perceive itself to be affected by the project.
why_it_matters: Stakeholder identification is an initiation focus and a common source of exam scenarios.
example: Patients, nurses, IT, compliance, and vendors are stakeholders in a healthcare software project.
exam_trap: Only considering 'customers' as stakeholders; regulators and internal teams also count.

title: Stakeholder identification
definition: Identifying stakeholders means discovering who has interests, influence, or impact related to the project and documenting them.
why_it_matters: PMP scenarios frequently start with missed stakeholders causing late surprises.
example: Including cybersecurity early prevents late-stage rework when security controls are required.
exam_trap: Treating stakeholder lists as one-time; you must update as the project evolves.

title: Stakeholder register
definition: A stakeholder register is a document listing stakeholders and key details such as role, interest, influence, and engagement expectations.
why_it_matters: The register is a common 'what document should you update?' answer choice.
example: Register includes the compliance officer’s influence level and required review checkpoints.
exam_trap: Confusing stakeholder register with communications plan; register identifies people, plan defines communications.

title: Power/interest grid
definition: A power/interest grid classifies stakeholders based on their authority (power) and their level of concern (interest) to guide engagement strategies.
why_it_matters: PMP tests stakeholder engagement strategy choices based on classification.
example: A CFO (high power, low interest) wants summary updates, not daily details.
exam_trap: Sending the same communication to everyone—tailor engagement level to stakeholder needs.

title: PM knowledge areas (overview)
definition: Knowledge areas group related project management processes such as scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, and stakeholder management.
key_points: Not a process group; it’s a classification
Used to organize planning and control
why_it_matters: PMP often checks your conceptual map of where work belongs and how areas interact.
example: A scope change affects schedule and cost; integration coordinates the trade-offs.
exam_trap: Treating knowledge areas as chronological steps; they overlap throughout the project.

title: Integration management (early focus)
definition: Integration management ensures project work is coordinated; early integration includes developing the charter and aligning objectives with strategy.
key_points: Develop Project Charter is an integration process
why_it_matters: Initiation questions often tie charter work to integration management.
example: PM ensures charter objectives align with portfolio strategy and constraints before detailed planning begins.
exam_trap: Assuming integration is only about change control—it's broader.

title: Enterprise environmental factors (EEFs)
definition: EEFs are internal/external conditions that influence or constrain the project, such as regulations, culture, marketplace, and infrastructure.
why_it_matters: PMP tests whether you can identify influences that come from the environment vs organizational assets.
example: A new data privacy law forces requirements for consent and data retention.
exam_trap: Calling internal templates an EEF; templates are OPAs (organizational process assets).

title: Organizational process assets (OPAs)
definition: OPAs are internal resources such as templates, policies, lessons learned, and historical information used to guide project work.
why_it_matters: PMP frequently contrasts OPAs vs EEFs in initiation/planning questions.
example: Using the organization’s charter template and historical schedule data from similar projects.
exam_trap: Treating laws/regulations as OPAs—they are EEFs.

title: Constraints: triple constraint perspective
definition: Schedule, cost, and scope are interdependent; changing one constraint usually affects at least one other and potentially quality/risk.
key_points: Quality and risk are impacted by constraint changes
why_it_matters: Questions often ask what the PM should do when a sponsor imposes a new deadline or budget cap.
example: Shortening schedule may require more cost (overtime) or reduced scope.
exam_trap: Agreeing to impossible constraints without negotiating scope/cost trade-offs.

title: Requirements vs solution design
definition: Requirements describe what is needed; solution design determines how to meet those requirements and is refined later.
why_it_matters: PMP scenarios often include stakeholders prescribing a solution; the PM should clarify needs first.
example: Stakeholder asks for 'a new app'; PM probes for underlying requirement: 'mobile access to scheduling.'
exam_trap: Locking in a solution before understanding requirements and constraints.

title: Constraints: mandated technology
definition: Sometimes sponsors or governance mandate tools/vendors; the PM treats this as a constraint and plans around it while managing expectations.
why_it_matters: Exam questions test whether you document constraints and adjust planning rather than arguing after the fact.
example: Company mandates Azure; PM ensures architecture and budget align with that constraint.
exam_trap: Treating mandated tech as optional or changing it without approval.

title: PM role in selection vs authorization
definition: Project selection is typically done by portfolio/governance; initiation authorizes via charter, after which the PM leads planning and execution.
key_points: PM may provide input but usually does not decide
why_it_matters: Clarifies who decides to start projects and what the PM can do at each stage.
example: Portfolio board approves project; sponsor issues charter; PM begins detailed planning.
exam_trap: Assuming PM can start or cancel projects unilaterally.

title: Stakeholder engagement strategy (initiation)
definition: Early engagement focuses on identifying key stakeholders, understanding expectations, and setting communication/participation approaches appropriate to influence.
why_it_matters: Many project failures start with stakeholder resistance—PMP tests proactive engagement.
example: Meet with department heads to clarify how the new workflow impacts staff and capture concerns.
exam_trap: Delaying stakeholder conversations until planning is complete, causing rework.

title: PM appointment timing
definition: Best practice is to assign the PM as early as possible (during initiation) to influence charter, stakeholder identification, and early planning.
why_it_matters: PMP questions may ask when PM should be assigned—early is the typical best answer.
example: PM participates in charter workshops to ensure milestones and constraints are realistic.
exam_trap: Assigning PM after planning is done; unrealistic commitments may already be made.

title: Project boundaries
definition: Project boundaries define what is in-scope and out-of-scope at a high level during initiation, setting expectations before detailed scope definition.
key_points: Clarifies what the project will not do
why_it_matters: Scope boundary misunderstandings commonly create conflict; PMP tests early clarity.
example: Charter states it will implement patient portal for appointments but not redesign billing.
exam_trap: Using vague boundaries like 'modernize systems' without excluding major adjacent work.

title: High-level risks (initiation)
definition: High-level risks are early, broad uncertainties identified during initiation to inform feasibility and charter decisions.
key_points: Often tied to assumptions and constraints
Not a full risk register yet
Used to inform go/no-go and early planning
why_it_matters: PMP tests that you identify risks early and include them at a high level in initiation artifacts.
example: Risk: vendor contract approval may take 8–10 weeks, threatening schedule constraint.
exam_trap: Waiting until execution to identify risks or ignoring risk because it’s 'too early.'

title: PM authority vs responsibility
definition: Responsibility is the accountability for outcomes; authority is the power to make decisions and allocate resources—misalignment creates friction.
why_it_matters: Scenario questions often describe a PM held accountable without authority; the right move is clarify/escalate appropriately.
example: PM is responsible for delivery but cannot secure SME time; escalates to sponsor to resolve priorities.
exam_trap: Trying to 'power through' without resolving authority gaps, leading to ongoing delays.

title: Requirements sources
definition: Requirements come from business documents, stakeholder needs, regulations, and operational constraints; initiation clarifies sources and ownership.
why_it_matters: PMP tests whether you identify the right stakeholders and sources before documenting requirements.
example: Compliance defines data retention; operations defines workflow needs; users define usability needs.
exam_trap: Only gathering requirements from loud stakeholders and missing regulatory/operational sources.

title: Initiation deliverables vs planning deliverables
definition: Initiation deliverables authorize and frame the project; planning deliverables create baselines and detailed plans for execution and control.
key_points: Planning: PM plan, baselines, detailed requirements
Different level of detail and purpose
why_it_matters: This distinction appears across many PMP questions about 'what do you do next?'
example: After charter approval, the PM develops the project management plan and scope baseline.
exam_trap: Skipping initiation artifacts and jumping to baselines without authorization.
